title: Drill Music
summary: By the mid-2010s, drill had splintered into regional variants, most notably Brooklyn Drill (also called NY Drill) and UK Drill.
content:
Drill music emerged in Chicago's South Side around 2010, pioneered by artists like Chief Keef, Lil Durk, and King Louie. The genre takes its name from the street slang "drill," meaning to fight or retaliate. The sound is defined by its aggressive 808 bass, sliding hi-hats, and ominous melodies, often produced by key figures like Young Chop and DJ L.

By the mid-2010s, drill had splintered into regional variants, most notably Brooklyn Drill (also called NY Drill) and UK Drill. Brooklyn Drill, spearheaded by Pop Smoke, Fivio Foreign, and Sheff G, incorporates a more melodic flow and faster tempo. UK Drill, originating in Brixton, London, developed its own distinct sound with artists like Digga D, Unknown T, and Headie One.

Drill has been controversial due to its association with gang violence and criminal activity, leading to censorship and police scrutiny in both the US and UK. Despite this, it remains one of the most influential hip-hop subgenres of the 2020s.

title: New York City Drill
summary: The NY Drill sound is characterized by its faster tempo (around 140-150 BPM), heavy use of sliding 808s, and a more melodic, rhythmic flow compared to Chicago drill.
content:
New York City Drill emerged around 2018 as a distinct regional offshoot of Chicago drill. The sound was pioneered by Brooklyn artists like Pop Smoke, Sheff G, and Fivio Foreign, with production from 808Melo, AXL Beats, and others.

The NY Drill sound is characterized by its faster tempo (around 140-150 BPM), heavy use of sliding 808s, and a more melodic, rhythmic flow compared to Chicago drill. Lyrically, it often references Brooklyn's gang culture, particularly the rivalry between the Crips and Bloods-influenced sets in the borough.

Pop Smoke's breakout hit "Welcome to the Party" (2019) brought the sound to global attention. Following his tragic death in 2020, the genre continued to grow through artists like Fivio Foreign, Sleepy Hallow, Sheff G, and 22Gz.

Drill culture in NYC is deeply intertwined with the city's gang landscape. Groups like the Woo (associated with Pop Smoke), the Choo (Chosen Few), and the Crips/Bloods sets in Brooklyn, Queens, and the Bronx form the backdrop of much of the music's narrative.

title: Chicago Drill
summary: The Chicago drill sound is notably darker and slower than its NYC counterpart, with tempos typically around 120-130 BPM.
content:
Chicago drill music originated around 2010-2012 on the South and West Sides of Chicago. Artists like Chief Keef, Lil Durk, King Louie, and Fredo Santana, along with producers like Young Chop, defined the sound that would influence the entire genre.

The Chicago drill sound is notably darker and slower than its NYC counterpart, with tempos typically around 120-130 BPM. Young Chop's production style—minimalist, with heavy 808 kicks and eerie melodies—set the template.

Lyrical content in Chicago drill is starkly autobiographical, reflecting the realities of life in Chicago's most marginalized neighborhoods. Songs often reference specific housing projects ("the projects"), street corners, and gang affiliations, particularly the divide between the Gangster Disciples and Black Disciples.

Notable early drill anthems include Chief Keef's "I Don't Like" and "Love Sosa," Lil Durk's "Dis Ain't What U Want," and King Louie's "Valley of Kings." The genre faced significant controversy and censorship, with some songs being linked to escalating gang violence.

title: California
summary: A growing archive documenting California's gang culture, sets, alliances, and history across Los Angeles, the Bay Area, and beyond.
content:
California is home to a vast and deeply rooted gang culture spanning from Los Angeles to the San Francisco Bay Area, San Diego, and the Central Valley. The state's gang landscape includes established organizations like the Crips, Bloods, Sureños, Norteños, and various independent sets that have influenced street culture worldwide.

This archive aims to document California's gang structures, alliances, rivalries, and notable figures in a format similar to the drillpedia entries for NYC and Chicago.
location: Los Angeles, San Francisco Bay Area, San Diego, California

title: UK Drill
summary: Musically, UK Drill is characterized by its distinctive sliding 808 pattern, syncopated hi-hat rolls, and the "drill flow" — a rhythmic, almost spoken delivery.
content:
UK Drill emerged around 2012-2014 in Brixton, London, pioneered by groups like 150, OFB (Original Farm Boys), and individuals like Digga D, Unknown T, and Headie One. The genre adapts the Chicago drill template into a uniquely British context.

Musically, UK Drill is characterized by its distinctive sliding 808 pattern, syncopated hi-hat rolls, and the "drill flow" — a rhythmic, almost spoken delivery. Production styles vary, but the sound is generally more minimalist and rhythm-focused than its American counterparts.

Lyrically, UK drill artists reference postcode rivalries, road life, and the realities of growing up in London's council estates. The genre has faced intense scrutiny from UK police and government, with some songs being used as evidence in criminal trials. This has led to widespread censorship on platforms like YouTube and Spotify.

Despite these challenges, UK Drill has achieved mainstream success. Artists like Central Cee, Digga D, and Headie One have crossed over into the mainstream while maintaining their drill sound.

title: Pop Smoke
summary: His debut mixtape "Meet the Woo" (2019) and its sequel "Meet the Woo 2" (2020) established him as a leading figure in the New York rap scene.
content:
Pop Smoke was born on July 20, 1999, in Brooklyn, New York. He rose to fame in 2019 with his breakout single "Welcome to the Party," produced by 808Melo. His deep, gravelly voice and energetic delivery quickly became the hallmark of the Brooklyn Drill sound.

His debut mixtape "Meet the Woo" (2019) and its sequel "Meet the Woo 2" (2020) established him as a leading figure in the New York rap scene. His posthumous debut album "Shoot for the Stars, Aim for the Moon" (2020) was a commercial and critical success, debuting at number one on the Billboard 200.

Pop Smoke's music frequently referenced his affiliation with the Woo, a subset of the Crips in Canarsie, Brooklyn. His lyrics painted vivid pictures of street life in Brooklyn while also showcasing his versatility on tracks like "What You Know Bout Love" and "For the Night."

On February 19, 2020, Pop Smoke was tragically killed during a home invasion in Los Angeles at the age of 20. His legacy continues to influence drill music worldwide, with his sound being adopted by artists across the globe.

title: Fivio Foreign
summary: His debut album "B.
content:
Fivio Foreign was born on March 29, 1990, in Brooklyn, New York. He gained recognition in 2019 with his feature on "Welcome to the Party" remix alongside Pop Smoke. His breakout single "Big Drip" (2019) became an anthem of the Brooklyn Drill movement.

His debut album "B.I.B.L.E." (2022), executively produced by Kanye West, showcased his versatility and cemented his place in drill history. The album featured collaborations with Kanye West, Alicia Keys, Quavo, and Ne-Yo.

Fivio's style is characterized by his punchy, rhythmic delivery and catchy drill flows. He is known for representing Brooklyn's gang culture in his music, often referencing specific sets and rivalries while emphasizing that drill music is documentation rather than glorification.

He continues to be one of the leading voices in New York drill, collaborating with artists across genres and helping to evolve the sound beyond its Brooklyn origins.

title: Brooklyn Gang Culture
summary: The most prominent affiliations include the Crips and Bloods, transplanted from Los Angeles but evolved into distinctly New York organizations.
content:
Brooklyn's gang culture is a complex tapestry of national affiliations, local sets, and neighborhood allegiances that forms the backdrop of much NYC drill music. Unlike Chicago's more established gang structure, Brooklyn's landscape is characterized by fractured sets and shifting alliances.

The most prominent affiliations include the Crips and Bloods, transplanted from Los Angeles but evolved into distinctly New York organizations. Specific sets like the Woo (Canarsie Crips), Choo (Chosen Few), and various Bloods sets are frequently referenced in drill lyrics.

Housing projects (NYCHA developments) play a central role in Brooklyn's gang culture. Developments like the Van Dyke Houses, Pink Houses, Lafayette Gardens, and Marcy Projects serve as territorial boundaries and community centers simultaneously.

It is important to note that this documentation serves as a research resource for understanding the cultural context of drill music. The information presented reflects historical and sociological observation, not endorsement of criminal activity. Many artists have spoken about using their music as a means of escaping the environments they describe.

title: Chicago Gang Culture
summary: The South Side and West Side of Chicago are particularly associated with drill music.
content:
Chicago's gang culture has a long and well-documented history dating back to the early 20th century. The modern landscape is dominated by powerful organizations like the Gangster Disciples (GDs), Black Disciples (BDs), Vice Lords, and Latin Kings, among others.

The South Side and West Side of Chicago are particularly associated with drill music. The divide between the GDs and BDs is a recurring theme in Chicago drill lyrics, with artists referencing specific blocks, intersections, and housing projects like the Robert Taylor Homes, Stateway Gardens, and Cabrini-Green.

Chicago's gang culture differs from other cities in its organizational structure. The city's gangs are often more hierarchical and established, with generations of family involvement creating deep-rooted traditions and rivalries.

This documentation serves purely as historical and cultural research. Understanding this context is essential for appreciating the art form that emerged from these environments. Many drill artists have used their platform to speak about the cycles of poverty and violence in their communities.

title: Chief Keef
summary: His debut studio album "Finally Rich" (2012) featured the hit "Love Sosa" and cemented his influence on the genre.
content:
Chief Keef was born on August 15, 1995, in Chicago, Illinois. Rising to fame as a teenager in 2012, his songs "I Don't Like" and "Love Sosa" became viral hits that defined the early drill sound. Produced by Young Chop, these tracks established the template for drill music worldwide.

His debut studio album "Finally Rich" (2012) featured the hit "Love Sosa" and cemented his influence on the genre. Despite his young age, Keef's deep voice, deadpan delivery, and unfiltered lyrics about life in Chicago's Parkway Gardens ("O-Block") resonated with a global audience.

Chief Keef's impact extends far beyond his own discography. His sound influenced a generation of rappers from Chicago to London to Sydney. Artists like Lil Durk, King Von, and even Pop Smoke have cited his influence.

His career has been marked by legal troubles and controversy, particularly surrounding lyrics that authorities claim incited violence. However, his defenders argue that his music is documentary in nature, simply reflecting the reality of his environment.

title: Lil Durk
summary: He founded the collective Only the Family (OTF), which has launched the careers of several artists including the late King Von, Booka600, and Doodie Lo.
content:
Lil Durk was born on October 19, 1992, in Chicago, Illinois. Growing up in Englewood, he was exposed to street life at a young age. His 2013 single "Dis Ain't What U Want" brought him widespread attention and established him as a leading voice in Chicago drill.

He founded the collective Only the Family (OTF), which has launched the careers of several artists including the late King Von, Booka600, and Doodie Lo. OTF represents a family-oriented approach to the drill scene, emphasizing loyalty and brotherhood.

Durk's music evolved from raw drill anthems to more introspective, melodic work. His album "The Voice" (2020) and the collaborative project with Lil Baby "The Voice of the Heroes" (2021) showcased his versatility and commercial appeal.

Like his peers, Durk's music is deeply rooted in Chicago's gang culture, specifically the Black Disciples affiliation of his neighborhood. He has been open about using his platform to document life in Chicago's most dangerous neighborhoods while trying to create opportunities for those around him.

title: Makk Balla Family
summary: MBF (Makk Balla Family), also known as the Maybakks (〽️🐝), was founded in 2001 by two youths in the Webster Houses / Washington Ave Projects (the area from 171st-168th St and 3rd Ave to Webster Ave, which is considered their motherland).
content:
Emojis:
〽️ 🐝
===
Explanation:
MBF (Makk Balla Family), also known as the Maybakks (〽️🐝), was founded in 2001 by two youths in the Webster Houses / Washington Ave Projects (the area from 171st-168th St and 3rd Ave to Webster Ave, which is considered their motherland). This set was originally initiated by Larry O and originated as a spin-off of the 59 Brims, who left the UBN to form the NYBBA (New York Blood Brim Army). MBF briefly joined the NYBBA on July 7th, 2003, but the alliance did not last long, and they were eventually kicked out. Between 2009 and 2012, a wave of mass indictments hitting the YBz (Young Bosses) in the Bronx caused almost all of their sets to flip to Makk Balla Brim. This momentum also extended their ongoing feud with the YGz and made the group's name go viral. However, not long ago, MBF was expelled from the Brim alliance. Some sources state this happened because the Brims viewed the rapid growth of the Makks as a "virus." Meanwhile, other sources suggest there was an element of jealousy, as MBF had the potential to overpower all other Brim sets since almost everyone preferred to turn Makk at the time. After breaking away from the Brims, they changed their name to MBF, established new internal rules ("whips"), and frequently engaged in internal conflicts among themselves as well as with other Bloods sets—including linking up with the GDs to go against other Hats. Currently, the majority of the Makk lineups still identify as Bloods (🅱️🩸), though not all of them do, and lowkey MBF has become one of the largest gangs in the city.

===
Explanation:
WildCard Crips (🚾🃏♿) aka Wildcard 90s Crips aka Decatur Crip Gang (DCG) were created in Kingsbridge Heights in 2012. Their motherland is 193 & Creston, and they started at E 194th - E 196th / Decatur Ave. The first Crips from this side jacked 94Gz as their block. DCG was later renamed to "E/S Rollin 90s Neighborhood Wild Card Crip", flipping from the 3x/Gangster card to the 2x/Neighborhood card under the Rollin 80s Rocc Gang Neighborhood Crip. This flip caused backlash and a minor beef with the now inactive Rollin 50s Neighborhood Crip (E 196th St / Decatur Ave). The set operates across Slattery, Kingsbridge, Reyway, 165, and more.

title: Gangster Disciples
summary: The Gangster Disciples (GD) and their offshoots are a highly influential organization originally founded in the South Side of Chicago during the late 1960s by Larry Hoover and David Barksdale.
content:
Emojis:
🔱🔯/🖤💙/7️⃣4️⃣
===
Explanation:
The Gangster Disciples (GD) and their offshoots are a highly influential organization originally founded in the South Side of Chicago during the late 1960s by Larry Hoover and David Barksdale. Operating under the Folk Nation alliance, they utilize core symbols such as the six-pointed Star of David and the upward-pointing pitchfork. While some listed below are direct subsets of GD, others are independent or distinct Folk Nation families that share the "Disciple" moniker and history.
===
Alliance:
Folk Nation (🔱/🔯)
===
Founder:
Larry Hoover (👑🔱), David Barksdale 🕊️ (👑🔱)
===
Allies:
Various Folk Nation sets, including sections of the Black Disciples (historically/selectively), Simon City Royals, and La Raza.
===
Enemies:
People Nation gangs (Vice Lords, Latin Kings), Black Disciples (frequent active feuds), and various renegade/opposing street factions.
===
Subsets:
Insane Gangster Disciples (⚡🔱🔯/♊🔱)
Outlaw Gangster Disciples (⭕🔱🔯)
BarNoneCrazy Gangster Disciples (📶🔱🔯)
Maniac Gangster Disciples (♏🔱✡️)
Maniac Latin Disciples (♏🩵✡️)
Satan Disciples (💛😈🔱)
Spanish Gangster Disciples (🩵✡️)
YLO Disciples (🔱♏🩵🔯)
NoLove74 Gangster Disciples (7️⃣4️⃣💔🖤🔱)
74Ram Gangster Disciples (7️⃣4️⃣🐏🖤🔱)
===

title: JayRipK 🕊️
summary: 13 Year Old Jaryan ''JayRipK'' Elliot (✌🏾🎱🎯/3rdSide) was killed on July 11th 2021 by Tako Gunz 🔐 (⛪/80h) & Squeeze 🔐 (🐺🎩🅱️/80h).
content:
Explanation:
On July 11th 2021, 13 Year Old Jaryan ''JayRipK'' Elliot 🕊️ (✌🏾🎱🎯/3rdSide) and his friend were walking on E 187th St, when a car pulled up on JayRipK and his friend. Suspect later identified as Tako Gunz 🔐 (⛪/80h) rolled down the window and started shooting at JayRipK hitting him in the leg and the driver later identified as Squeeze 🔐 (🐺🎩🅱️/80h) drove away. JayRipK was trying to escape and hid into local cafe shop ''Angels Café'' where he bled out and died on the floor of the Cafe Shop. Few minutes after his death Kay Flock (⛪/SevSide) came to the Cafe and watched JayRipK's body lying on the floor.
===
Killed By:
Tako Gunz 🔐 (⛪/80h), Squeeze 🔐 (🐺🎩🅱️/80h)

title: Notti Osama 🕊️
summary: 14-year-old Ethan ''Notti Osama'' Reyes (🎱Ⓜ️♿️ ex-🔝🅾🐝,⭕✌🏾/SMF & SHGTM) was stabbed to death on July 9, 2022 by KdotKeepClickin (🏁 ex-💚🍍/9Raq ex-700).
content:
Explanation:
Notti Osama (🎱Ⓜ️♿️ ex-🔝🅾🐝,⭕✌🏾/SMF & SHGTM) , born Ethan Reyes, was a 14-year-old drill rapper from New York who was stabbed to death on July 9, 2022. The fatal altercation occurred on a subway platform at the 137th Street–City College station in Manhattan during a physical confrontation with a 15-year-old rival. During the physical fight, Reyes cornered the other teen with a broomstick. The rival retaliated with a knife, stabbing Reyes in the abdomen, which ultimately proved fatal. The 15-year-old was initially charged with murder, but Manhattan prosecutors ultimately dropped all charges, concluding there was sufficient evidence that he acted in self-defense.
===
Killed By:
KdotKeepClickin (🏁 ex-💚🍍/9Raq ex-700)

title: Wasca 🕊️
summary: 24-year-old Hwascar ''OY Wasca'' Hernandez (💚🍍/SHGTM) was shot and killed on December 16, 2021 by Kay Flock 🔒 (⛪️4️⃣5️⃣✊🏿🔥/SevSide).
content:
Explanation:
Wasca/Hwascar ''OY Wasca'' Hernandez (💚🍍/SHGTM) was a 24-year-old man who was tragically shot and killed on December 16, 2021 in Harlem, Manhattan. The fatal event unfolded at a barbershop near 151st Street and Amsterdam Avenue. According to police and surveillance reports, the suspect walked past the barbershop, and Hernandez ran outside to confront him. The altercation escalated when the suspect opened fire, striking Hernandez in the neck and back. Hernandez was subsequently rushed to Mount Sinai Hospital, where he died from his injuries. Police identified drill rapper Kay Flock (born Kevin Perez) as the main suspect. After an investigation, Perez turned himself in to the NYPD on December 23, 2021. Following his arrest, the case escalated to the federal level. In February 2023, Kay Flock and seven others were federally indicted on racketeering charges related to the Sevside/DOA gang, and the federal government took over his murder case. He faces federal first-degree murder charges, with prosecutors pointing to gang violence. Federal prosecutors initially considered the death penalty for Perez, but officially withdrew that decision in July 2023.
===
Killed By:
Kay Flock 🔒 (⛪️4️⃣5️⃣✊🏿🔥/SevSide)
location: 151st St & Amsterdam Ave, Harlem, Manhattan, NYC

content:

The 68 Playboy Hustler Crips (68PBHC), also once called Baby Hoovers, are part of the Playboy Hustler Crips that have 5 main sets and fall under the Hustler Crip and Gangster Crip (Trays 3X) Cards. Their territory is around W 68th Street & Figueroa Street in South Los Angeles. Their area especially around Figueroa Street is mostly known as a hotspot for prostitution. This is because it is one of the few sections not claimed by HCG which makes it more attractive to pimps who do not want to be taxed by the Hoover gangs. They are not affiliated with PBGC in West LA. Though compared to other sets in their area not much has been heard about PBHC in recent years.

title: W/S 107 Hoover Criminals Gang / Selos
summary: The 107 Hoover Criminals Gang (107HCG), also known as Selos, are from 107th Street & Hoover / Normandie in South LA, founded in 1975.
content:
Explanation:
The 107 Hoover Criminals Gang (107HCG), also known as Selos, is a Hoover Criminals Gang set from 107th Street & Hoover / Normandie Avenue in South Los Angeles, founded in 1975. 107HCG is one of the more active and notorious Hoover sets, though ongoing feuds with the Rollin 100s, 60s, 90s, 984MSMC, DLGB, and SXL13 have resulted in many of their members either becoming incarcerated or deceased. Their direct proximity across the avenue from UGBC was the main factor in early conflicts that led to the now five decades long deadly war with the Rollin 100s. The beef alone is responsible for the Westmont district being nicknamed Death Alley by Los Angeles Times as the neighborhood has consistently held the highest murder rate in the county since the early 2000s. Like most other Hoover gangs many of their younger members are of Hispanic/Mexican descent. This set is known for rapper Jap5.

title: W/S 107 Original Blocc Crips (107OBC)
summary: 107 Original Blocc Crips (107OBC) are a Rollin' 100's set from 107th Street (Western to Denker) in South Los Angeles, part of the Blue Flame Gang alliance.
location: 107th Street (Western to Denker Ave), South Los Angeles, California
content:
Explanation:
The 107 Original Blocc Crips (107OBC) are a Rollin' 100's set from 107th Street from Western to Denker Avenue in South Los Angeles. They are part of the Rollin' 100's alliance but also have their own smaller alliance within it called Blue Flame Gang (BFG) aka Fleezy Gang. They infight with some Rollin' 100's sets, especially OEF / 1e11um5. 107OBC, 113OBC, and UGBC are so closely allied that they are almost indistinguishable and are considered the same set by most people in the SCLA/Westmont area. They are also cliqued up with 104HTHC. Their main rivals are the Hoover gangs located across Normandie Avenue — this beef is one of the deadliest in LA County history. They have also been beefing heavily with 111NHC and 115NHC despite all being under the Rollin' 100's Crip umbrella.

location: 103rd-107th Street (Denker to Normandie Ave), South Los Angeles, California
content:
Explanation:
The Underground Blocc Crips (UGBC) are a Rollin' 100's set from 103rd Street to 107th Street, from Denker to Normandie Avenue in South Los Angeles. They are part of the Rollin' 100's alliance but also have their own smaller alliance within it called Blue Flame Gang (BFG) aka Fleezy Gang. They infight with some Rollin' 100's sets, especially OEF / 1e11um5. UGBC's main clique is 105th, though they also claim 103rd, 106th, and 107th. This is one of the more active and historically notorious Rollin' 100's sets, known for their decades-long beef with 83GC and 107HCG that has resulted in many homicides in the Westmont area. UGBC as part of BFG also beefs heavily with 111NHC and 115NHC, who also claim Rollin' 100's but are on the other side of Western Avenue. The area east on 105th and Normandie used to be claimed by an allied gang called 105 Gangster Crips (105GC), though this set died out by the 2000s due to pressure from 107HCG.

content:
Explanation:
Budlong Gangster Crips (BLGC) are a Rollin' 100's set based on 102nd and Budlong Avenue in South Los Angeles. They were originally an independent set with close ties to UGBC and HTHC, as well as a longstanding rivalry with 83GC. They eventually joined the Rollin' 100's alliance in the early 2000's (around 2004/2005). Despite having Gangster Crips in their name, they are not affiliated with the Gangster Crip / 3x / Trey card and instead claim 2x / Neighborhood Crip. This is because they came in under UGBC and operate under the Deuces card.
===

Rollin' 100's (Alliance)
===
Allies:
UGBC, 104HTHC
===

title: W/S 83 Gangster Crips / Hell Gang (83GC)
summary: 83 Gangster Crips (83GC/ETGC), also known as Hell Gang, are the largest Black gang in LA by territory and the founding Gangster Crip set, originally formed as Original Gangster Crips (OGC).
location: W 83rd Street area, South Los Angeles, California
content:
Explanation:
The 83 (Eight-Trey) Gangster Crips (83GC/ETGC), also known as Hell Gang, are the largest Black gang in LA as far as territory goes, though the 60s and a few other gangs outnumber them in membership. 83GC were originally formed around W 83rd Street as a splinter gang off the Original West Side Crips that included Stanley Tookie Williams, Big Bob the Hawk, Melvin Farmer, Judson Bacot, Angelo Barefoot Pookie White and Mad Dog. They were originally founded as Original Gangster Crips (OGC) before rebranding to 83GC. 83GC is considered the founding Gangster Crip set and historically the most influential and respected set under GC/3X, making them the flagship Gangster Crip set that heavily influences GC politics nationwide. Their hood day is August 3rd. 83GC are mostly known for their beefs with the Rollin' 60s and 90s that have been going on for over four decades, which led to the major split and animosity between most GC and NHC sets across LA County and SoCal. 83GC is one of the only Crip sets on good terms with IFGB, as they don't beef with Damu gangs. Their main cliques include Nutty Northside, Bacc West, Original Southside, and Far West.
===

Nutty Northside (Clique)
Bacc West (Clique)
Original Southside (Clique)
Far West (Clique)

title: W/S 99 Mafia Crips / 9'ers (99MC)
summary: 99 Mafia Crips (99MC), also known as the 9'ers, are the only Mafia Crip set on the West side of South Central, based on W 99th Street & Budlong Ave.
location: W 99th Street & Budlong Avenue, South Los Angeles, California
content:
Explanation:
The 99 Mafia Crips (99MC), better known as the 9'ers, hold the distinction of being the only Mafia Crip set operating on the West side of South Central — the rest are concentrated on the East side and the Inland Empire. Based on W 99th Street and Budlong Avenue, they may be small in numbers but are one of the most hated gangs in the Westmont area. They are rumored to have been responsible for the 2014 shooting death of 17-year-old Keonta "Blue Flame" Daniels, an incident that reportedly led the Blocc Crips to adopt the Flame Gang name. Their longstanding war with 83GC eventually led to a truce, though tensions still linger. Unlike their Mafia Crip counterparts on the East side, they don't get along with NHC and BC cards and are known to clash with most of the Rollin' 100's.
===

===

location: Bacc West territory (83GC turf), South Los Angeles, California
content:
Explanation:
Evil Klan 13 (EK13) are a small Sureño gang whose territory sits inside what is primarily considered Bacc West, the western clique of 83 Gangster Crips turf in South Los Angeles. Despite sharing ground with one of the largest Crip sets in the city, EK13 hold their own as a separate entity. They mainly beef with other Sureño rivals like XV3, SSL13, and NC13, as well as 83GC and 99MC.

title: South Side Locos 13 (SSL13)
summary: South Side Locos 13 (SSL13) are a small, barely active Sureño gang located in Bacc West (83GC) territory in South Los Angeles.
location: Bacc West territory (83GC turf), South Los Angeles, California
content:
Explanation:
South Side Locos 13 (SSL13) are a small Sureño gang situated inside Bacc West, the western clique of 83 Gangster Crips turf. They have been barely active in recent years due to low recruitment — most Hispanic youths in the area choose to claim EK13, XV3, or 924 Hoover instead. Their main rivalries are with fellow Sureño sets EK13 and XV3, as well as 83GC and 924 Hoovers.

title: W/S 65 Menlo Neighborhood Crips (65MNHC)
summary: 65 Menlo Neighborhood Crips (65MNHC) are a Rollin' 70's set formerly known as Menlo Gangster Crips, based around 65th & Menlo in South Los Angeles.
location: 65th Street & Menlo Avenue, South Los Angeles, California
content:
Explanation:
The Menlo Neighborhood Crips (65MNHC) originally went by Menlo Gangster Crips (65MGC) before flipping to Neighborhood Crip when their beef with 83GC kicked off years ago. They are part of the Rollin' 70's and are cliqued up with 67NHC under the 6570 banner, with their main cliques being 65th and 70th. Their hood day is June 5th. With their rivals PBHC gradually fading over the years, MNHC has recently pushed east toward 65th & Figueroa — making them the only NHC set that can claim the blade as their turf. Despite calling themselves Rollin' 70's, rivals don't consider the title official since their territory doesn't extend south past 71st or Florence Avenue. They operate within Florencia territory and their main beefs are with the Hoover gangs, particularly 52HGC, 59HCG, and 74HCG.

title: W/S Harvard Park Brims (HPB)
summary: Harvard Park Brims (HPB), also known as 62 Brims or Six-Deuce Brims, are one of the oldest Blood gangs in LA predating the Crips, based around 61st-64th & Harvard Park.
location: 61st-64th Street & Harvard Park, South Los Angeles, California
content:
Explanation:
The Harvard Park Brims (HPB), also known as 62 Brims or Six-Deuce Brims (62B/SDB), are one of the oldest gangs in Los Angeles — they were around before the Crips ever formed. Based around 61st through 64th Streets near Harvard Park, they are part of the FHN (Fruit Harvard Ness) alliance and share their hood with the Florencia 13 Locos clique, who are considered allies. Their 59th Street clique went extinct years ago due to ongoing beef with the Rollin' 50's and 59HCG. The Slauson Super Mall sits within their set boundaries. HPB is the main Brim gang that has consistently warred with the Hoovers, particularly 52HGC and 59HCG, for decades. For this reason, they were one of the only Brim sets on the west side that didn't fully attach to the SMG movement during the early 2010s and remained truly CK. They are also known to beef with most Neighborhood Crip and Gangster Crip sets in the surrounding area.
===

title: W/S 58 Neighborhood Crips (58NHC)
summary: 58 Neighborhood Crips (58NHC), also known as Baby 60's or 5578NHC, are a Rollin' 50's set from W 58th Place in South Los Angeles.
location: W 58th Place, South Los Angeles, California
content:
Explanation:
The 58 Neighborhood Crips (58NHC), also known as Baby 60's or 5578NHC, are the smallest of the three sets that make up the Rollin' 50's and the most distant from the other two. Based on W 58th Place, they are extremely tight with the Rollin' 60's and have a long history of beefing with the Harvard Park Brims. In recent years their beef with the Rollin' 40's has also heated up. Despite being smaller in size, they hold their ground as part of the Rollin' 50's alliance alongside 55NHC and 57NHC.

content:
Explanation:
The 57 Neighborhood Crips (57NHC) are one of the three sets that form the Rollin' 50's, based on W 57th Street between Western and Normandie Avenue. title: W/S 55 Neighborhood Crips (55NHC)
summary: 55 Neighborhood Crips (55NHC) are one of the three sets forming the Rollin' 50's, based on W 55th St. The deepest of the three, made famous by rapper Crip Mac.
location: W 55th Street (Western to Normandie Ave), South Los Angeles, California
content:
Explanation:
The 55 Neighborhood Crips (55NHC) are one of the three sets that form the Rollin' 50's alongside 57NHC and 58NHC under the 5578NHC banner. Based on W 55th Street between Western and Normandie Avenue, they are the deepest of the three in terms of membership. This hood gained mainstream attention through viral rapper and vlogger Crip Mac, who is from here. Historically, 55NHC has been terrorized by VNGB, whose territory lies directly west off Western Avenue. They share the same rivalries as their Rollin' 50's counterparts, beefing hard with most Hoover sets, Gangster Crips, and the Harvard Park Brims.

title: W/S 51 Trouble Gangster Crips (51TGC)
summary: 51 Trouble Gangster Crips (51TGC), also known as 51 Halldale or Nothing But Trouble, are a former tagging crew turned Gangster Crip set from W 51st & Halldale. Allied with 52HGC to form 512GC.
location: W 51st Street & Halldale Avenue, South Los Angeles, California
content:
Explanation:
The 51 Trouble Gangster Crips (51TGC), also known as 51 Halldale or Nothing But Trouble (NBT), started out as a tagging crew before transitioning into a street gang over two decades ago. Based on W 51st Street and Halldale Avenue, they are seen as Baby Hoovers and are closely cliqued with 52HGC to form 512GC. They were among several crews from that era alongside Dirty Old Man, We Don't Care, Kings With Style, and others before making the jump to full gang status. Their main beefs are with the Rollin' 30's, 40's, 50's, and 60's Neighborhood Crips, as well as 984MSMC and Harvard Park Brims.

title: W/S Rollin 60's Neighborhood Crips / Score Gang (R60NHC)
summary: Rollin' 60's Neighborhood Crips (R60NHC), also known as Score Gang, are the largest Black gang in LA County by membership, made world-famous by rapper Nipsey Hussle.
location: Brynhurst Avenue area, South Los Angeles, California
content:
Explanation:
The Rollin' 60's Neighborhood Crips (R60NHC), also known as Score Gang or RSC, are the largest Black gang in Los Angeles County by membership and arguably the most famous African American gang in the world. Their large territory is divided into four main cliques: Overhill (aka Bacc Hood), Avenues, Front Hood, and the Dime Blocc, though most of their activity is concentrated around the apartment buildings along Brynhurst Avenue. They rose to worldwide prominence through rapper Nipsey Hussle, who was tragically gunned down in 2019 outside his Marathon Clothing Store on Crenshaw and Slauson by a fellow Rollin' 60's member. Historically their biggest rivals have been the Rollin' 40's, with whom they were once allied before a major falling out. They also beef with most Hoover and Gangster Crip sets across the city.

location: Centinela Park (Edward Vincent Jr. Park), Inglewood, California
content:
Explanation:
Centinela Park Family Gangster Bloods (CPFGB) are based around Edward Vincent Jr. Park, also known as Centinela Park, in Inglewood. They originally went by Centinela Park Pirus but are so close with IFGB that they eventually adopted the Family Gangster Blood name and are now often mentioned as one big set. Their main cliques are 64th, 65th, and 68th, and they are known for beefing heavily with the Rollin 60s.

summary: Neighborhood Pirus (NHP) are a Piru set based in Inglewood, hanging at Roger's Park. One of two NHP sets, the other being in Compton.
content:
Explanation:
Neighborhood Pirus (NHP) are one of two NHP sets in LA County — this one is based on the West Side of Inglewood, while the other is located in Compton. They hang around Roger's Park and the Inglewood Marketplace. This hood got its start from the Rollin 20s Neighborhood Bloods decades ago, which is why both sets use the same hand sign. They also have a significant number of Hispanic members. Their main cliques are 64th (Bix4), 500 Block, 700 Block, and 800 Block.

title: W/S Rollin' 30's Original Harlem Crips / Dirt Gang (R30OHC)
summary: Rollin' 30's Original Harlem Crips (R30OHC) aka Dirt Gang are the third largest Crip gang in LA behind the Rollin 60's and 83 Gangster Crips, based in the W 30th Streets / Exposition Park area.
location: W 30th Streets / Exposition Park area, South Los Angeles, California
content:
Explanation:
The Rollin' 30's Harlem Crips (R30OHC) aka Dirt Gang got their name from the Harlem God Fathers and their territory being in the W 30th Streets, originally founded as the Harlem Godfathers during the 1970s. This area was nicknamed "Harlem" due to the high concentration of Black/African-American families and businesses in Exposition Park. They are the third largest Crip gang in LA behind the Rollin 60's and 83 Gangster Crips. Most of their members are of Belizean descent. Even though they are Rollin', they aren't Neighborhood Crips, though they're still allied with sets under the 2X/NHC cards. They have five main cliques: Avenues, 35th, 37th, 39th, and Denker Park. Their main feuds have historically been with all bordering Blood gangs, more specifically the Rollin' 20's, FTB, CSB, JSB, and all other Blood gangs which is why they claim "BK/NSA" (No Slobs Allowed). They also beef with HPB, VNGB, IFGB, and all other Hoover gangs.
===

title: W/S Rollin' 90's Neighborhood Crips / XC / JO Gang (R90NHC)
summary: Rollin' 90's Neighborhood Crips (R90NHC), also known as XC or JO Gang (Jesse Owens Gang), are located in the Gramercy Park neighborhood of South LA.
location: Gramercy Park / Jesse Owens Park area, South Los Angeles, California
content:
Explanation:
The Rollin' 90's Neighborhood Crips (R90NHC), also known as XC or JO Gang (Jesse Owens Gang), are located in the Gramercy Park neighborhood and mostly hang around Jesse Owens Park. Their Hood Day is September 10th. Although being a Crip gang, they are known in recent years for putting on many non-Black youths, especially Hispanic youths, into their gang. R90NHC made headlines in 2017 when 18-year-old affluent white member Cameron "Milk" Terrell and three other teens were charged with the murder of a non-affiliate mistaken for being from 83GC. Their rivalry with 83GC dates back to the 1980s and ultimately led to the split between NHC and GC gangs in the city. They also feud heavily with IFGB and the Hoover gangs.

summary: 104 Hard Time Hustler Crips (104HTHC) are a set part of the Rollin' 100's, named after 104th Street and located in the Century Group Apartments.
location: Century Group Apartments (Century & Van Ness Ave), South Los Angeles, California
content:
Explanation:
The 104 Hard Time Hustler Crips (104HTHC) are a set part of the Rollin' 100's. They get their set name from 104th Street but are located in the Century Group Apartments off Century and Van Ness Avenue. This is the largest Hustler Crip set in the city, even though they are under the NHC card as well. Their main allies within the Rollin' 100's are the Blocc Crip gangs, which is why they currently beef with 111NHC despite them all being under the Rollin' 100's umbrella. They don't get into the infighting of the Rollin' 100's with BFG vs OEF. Their main feuds are with 83GC, 99MC, IFGB, CMGB, APG, NHP, MDS13, and sometimes the Rollin' 90's.

title: W/S 111 Neighborhood Crips (111NHC)
summary: 111 Neighborhood Crips (111NHC) are a set part of the Rollin' 100's, named after 111th Street and cliqued with 115NHC to form the OEF / 1e11um5 alliance.
location: 111th Street & Western Ave / Holly Park area, South Los Angeles, California
content:
Explanation:
The 111 Neighborhood Crips (111NHC) are a set part of the Rollin' 100's, named after 111th Street. They are said to be one of the original West side Neighborhood Crip gangs by name, having been around 111th and Western Avenue since the early 1970s. They hang out around the Holly Park area and are heavily cliqued up with 115NHC to form the 1e11um5 / OEF (One Eleven Five) alliance. They are currently in a major beef with the Blocc Crip gangs (OBC/UGBC, now allied as Blue Flame Gang) directly across the street within the Rollin' 100's, which has made the 108th/Western intersection and Westmont especially hot in recent years. Outside of the Rollin' 100's, they also beef with 83GC, HGC, SGC, IFGB, CMGB, CPB, APG, and the Hoover gangs.
===

title: Texas
summary: Texas has a growing drill scene, particularly in Houston, Dallas, and San Antonio, blending Southern hip-hop traditions with drill's hard-hitting sound.
content: Texas Drill emerged in the late 2010s, adapting the Chicago drill template to a Southern context. Houston has been the primary hub, with artists blending chopped-and-screwed influences with drill's characteristic 808 patterns. Key cities include Houston, Dallas, San Antonio, and Austin. The Texas drill scene is still developing compared to NYC or Chicago, but has been gaining traction through local collectives and regional beefs.
